hypothesis: Replacing the template-based natural language prompt generation with prompts authored by domain-naive human users will reveal a systematic performance degradation in CHROMA that correlates specifically with the linguistic complexity of constraint expressions (e.g., relative thickness comparisons, forbidden adjacencies, positional ordering), such that fine-tuning the frozen LLM encoder on a small set of domain-specific constraint phrasings—rather than increasing decoder depth—will close more than half of the resulting performance gap, demonstrating that the generalization bottleneck for out-of-distribution language shifts from decoder capacity to encoder comprehension when prompt distributions deviate from templated paraphrases.
